runs.interpretation: The IES for nondurable consumption in an annual macro-calibration setting for a representative U.S. household, measuring the percent change in consumption growth per 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption, treated as distinct from risk aversion.
citations: Hall (1988, JPE) – estimated IES close to zero using aggregate U.S. consumption and interest rate data, influential lower-bound anchor.
Vissing-Jørgensen & Attanasio (2003, NBER Macro Annual) – found IES around 0.3–0.8 for stockholders, higher than non-stockholders.
Bansal & Yaron (2004, JF) – long-run risks model calibrated with IES > 1 (≈1.5) to match equity premium, providing upper-end anchor.
runs.reasoningSummary: The IES for nondurable consumption is deeply contested. Hall (1988) found near-zero estimates from aggregate data, pulling the lower tail down. Micro-based studies (Vissing-Jørgensen, Attanasio) and structural asset-pricing models (Epstein-Zin, Bansal-Yaron) favor values in the 0.3–1.5 range. In annual macro calibrations separating IES from risk aversion (Epstein-Zin preferences), a common compromise is around 0.5, with a wide spread reflecting genuine empirical disagreement. I place the median at 0.5, with meaningful probability mass above 1 for asset-pricing-motivated calibrations and below 0.2 for traditional aggregate-data estimates.

runs.interpretation: The IES for nondurable consumption in an annual macro-calibration setting for a representative U.S. household, measuring the percent change in consumption growth per 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption, treated as distinct from risk aversion (e.g., as in Epstein-Zin preferences).
citations: Hall (1988, JPE) — found IES close to zero using aggregate consumption and interest rates, influential lower-bound anchor.
Bansal and Yaron (2004, JF) — long-run risks model calibrated with IES > 1 (around 1.5) to generate plausible asset pricing moments.
Vissing-Jørgensen and Attanasio (2003, JEEA) — stockholder IES estimates around 0.3–0.8 for nondurables, higher than Hall's aggregate estimates.
runs.reasoningSummary: The IES for nondurable consumption is one of the most debated parameters in macroeconomics. Hall (1988) found near-zero estimates using aggregate data. Micro-based studies (e.g., Vissing-Jørgensen) typically find values in the 0.3–0.8 range for stockholders/nondurables. Asset-pricing models (Bansal-Yaron long-run risks) require IES > 1 to match equity premium and risk-free rate. Annual macro calibrations commonly use values between 0.3 and 1.0; a modal calibration choice around 0.5 is common (e.g., used in many RBC/DSGE contexts). The distribution is right-skewed because some influential asset-pricing calibrations push it above 1, but most empirical estimates for nondurables land below 1. I set p50 = 0.5 reflecting the center of gravity of macro calibration practice, with meaningful probability mass from ~0.1 to ~1.5.

I place the bulk of the distribution between 0.2 and 1.0, with a long right tail reflecting models that require IES > 1.

runs.interpretation: IES for nondurable consumption in an annual macro-calibration setting for a representative U.S. household, defined as the percent change in consumption growth per 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption, treated as distinct from risk aversion.
citations: Hall (1988, JPE) — estimated IES close to zero using aggregate consumption and interest rates, often cited as evidence for low IES (~0.1 or less).
Vissing-Jørgensen & Attanasio (2003, Brookings) — found higher IES (~0.3–0.8) for stockholders using micro data on nondurables.
Epstein & Zin (1989, Econometrica) — recursive preferences separating IES from risk aversion; calibrations commonly use IES around 0.5–1.0 to match asset pricing facts.
runs.reasoningSummary: The IES for nondurable consumption is a contested parameter. Hall (1988) found estimates near zero using aggregate U.S. data. Micro-based studies (e.g., Vissing-Jørgensen) and asset-pricing calibrations (Epstein-Zin, Bansal-Yaron) tend to favor higher values in the 0.5–1.5 range, especially to explain the equity premium. In standard annual macro calibrations, a commonly adopted value is around 0.5 (or sometimes 1.0 for Bansal-Yaron style models). Balancing the low estimates from aggregate time-series regressions against the higher values needed to fit asset prices and preferred in modern DSGE models, my median belief is 0.5, with meaningful probability mass from ~0.1 to ~1.5.

runs.interpretation: The IES for nondurable consumption in an annual macro-calibration setting for a representative U.S. household, measuring the percent change in consumption growth per 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption, treated as distinct from risk aversion.
citations: Hall (1988, JPE) – estimated IES close to zero using aggregate U.S. consumption data, influencing a generation of low-IES calibrations.
Epstein & Zin (1989, Econometrica) – separated IES from risk aversion in recursive utility, motivating IES > 1 for asset pricing.
Bansal & Yaron (2004, JF) – long-run risks model calibrated with IES > 1 (≈1.5) to match equity premium, spurring debate about plausible IES range.
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature is deeply divided. Hall (1988) found IES near zero from aggregate consumption regressions. Many RBC calibrations use IES around 1 (log utility). Epstein-Zin recursive utility models and long-run risk models (Bansal-Yaron) favor IES > 1. Micro-based estimates (e.g., Attanasio & Weber) tend to be somewhat higher than Hall but still modest, often 0.3–0.8. In annual macro calibration, a consensus range of roughly 0.2–1.0 is common, with many workhorse models using 0.5 or 1.0. I place the median at 0.5, reflecting the moderate-IES camp that balances micro evidence and macro calibration practice, with substantial uncertainty on both sides.

I center my median at 0.5, reflecting the compromise between near-zero micro estimates and the higher values needed for asset pricing, with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly 0.1 to 1.5 at the 90% interval."}

runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical estimates of the IES span a wide range. Hall (1988) found near-zero IES using aggregate data. Subsequent work (Vissing-Jørgensen, Attanasio, and others) found higher values, especially for asset holders, often 0.3–1.0. In annual macro calibrations using Epstein-Zin preferences (e.g., long-run risk models by Bansal-Yaron), IES is often set around 1.5, but when calibrated to match consumption moments directly and not driven by asset-pricing discipline alone, values around 0.5 are common. The literature is genuinely dispersed; I center at 0.5 as a moderate consensus, with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly 0.1 to 1.5 at the 5th–95th percentile range.

In annual macro calibrations using Epstein-Zin preferences (e.g., long-run risk models by Bansal-Yaron), IES is often set around 1.5, but when calibrated to match consumption moments directly and not driven by asset-pricing discipline alone, values around 0.5 are common. The literature is genuinely dispersed; I center at 0.5 as a moderate consensus, with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly 0.1 to 1.5 at the 5th\u201395th percentile range."}
runs.prompt:
Answer from your current memory and background knowledge only.
Do not use tools, files, the web, code, or external resources.
Do not try to reconstruct a literature review or search for a consensus estimate.
Report the belief you currently endorse.

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Intertemporal elasticity of substitution
- Definition: Percent change in the growth rate of nondurable consumption from a 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption.
- Target interpretation: IES for nondurable consumption in annual macro calibration, distinct from risk aversion
- Population/context: Representative U.S. household in an annual macro-calibration setting
- Units: elasticity

Task:
1. Use exactly the target interpretation above. In `interpretation`, restate it briefly.
2. Give your subjective quantiles p05, p25, p50, p75, and p95 for this quantity.
3. Set `point_estimate` equal to `p50`.
4. Make the quantiles weakly increasing and numerically coherent.
5. In `citations`, list up to 3 source anchors from memory that influenced your belief. These are recall anchors only. If none come to mind confidently, return `[]`.
6. Keep `reasoning_summary` brief and substantive.

Return valid JSON only with exactly this shape:
{
  "interpretation": "...",
  "point_estimate": <number>,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": <number>,
    "p25": <number>,
    "p50": <number>,
    "p75": <number>,
    "p95": <number>
  },
  "citations": ["..."],
  "reasoning_summary": "..."
}

runs.interpretation: The IES for nondurable consumption in an annual macro-calibration setting for a representative U.S. household, measuring the percent change in consumption growth per 1 percent change in the intertemporal price of consumption, treated as distinct from risk aversion (as in Epstein-Zin preferences).
citations: Hall (1988, JPE) — estimated IES close to zero using aggregate U.S. consumption data, suggesting very low elasticity.
Bansal and Yaron (2004, JF) — long-run risks model calibrates IES > 1 (around 1.5) to match asset pricing moments.
Vissing-Jørgensen and Attanasio (2003, NBER/AER) — stockholder-level estimates of IES in the range 0.3–0.8, with nondurables closer to 0.5–0.8.
runs.reasoningSummary: The IES for nondurable consumption is one of the most contested parameters in macro-finance. Hall (1988) found values near zero using aggregate data. Micro-based and stockholder studies (Vissing-Jørgensen, Attanasio) tend to find values in the 0.3–0.8 range. Asset pricing models (Bansal-Yaron long-run risks) require IES > 1 to fit the equity premium and term structure simultaneously. Annual macro calibrations often split the difference, with many using values around 0.5 (e.g., standard RBC/New Keynesian models) or pushing toward 1–1.5 for asset-pricing-flavored calibrations. My median of 0.5 reflects the central tendency of empirical estimates for nondurable consumption; the wide spread from 0.1 to 1.5 captures genuine estimation uncertainty and model dependence.

Hall (1988) found values near zero using aggregate data. Micro-based and stockholder studies (Vissing-J\u00f8rgensen, Attanasio) tend to find values in the 0.3\u20130.8 range. Asset pricing models (Bansal-Yaron long-run risks) require IES > 1 to fit the equity premium and term structure simultaneously. Annual macro calibrations often split the difference, with many using values around 0.5 (e.g., standard RBC/New Keynesian models) or pushing toward 1\u20131.5 for asset-pricing-flavored calibrations. My median of 0.5 reflects the central tendency of empirical estimates for nondurable consumption; the wide spread from 0.1 to 1.5 captures genuine estimation uncertainty and model dependence."}
